title: Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index
subtitle: Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by [V-Dem](#dod:v-dem), based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).
caption:
Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index, 2025
Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by V-Dem, based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of the population living in poverty and the Electoral Democracy Index for countries in 2025. The vertical axis measures the share of the population in poverty, defined as living on less than $10 a day, ranging from 0% to over 80%. The horizontal axis represents the Electoral Democracy Index, a scale from 0 to 1 where higher values indicate more democratic systems. The data points are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A clear negative correlation is visible across the chart: countries with higher democracy scores generally show lower poverty rates. In the upper-left quadrant, nations with low democracy scores and high poverty include Burundi, Ethiopia, and Benin, where the share of the population in poverty exceeds 80%. Conversely, the bottom-right section contains countries with high democracy scores and low poverty, such as Australia, Chile, and Costa Rica, all falling below the 20% poverty line. Several countries occupy the middle ground; for instance, Indonesia and Georgia show moderate democracy scores around 0.5 to 0.6 with poverty rates between 60% and 70%. In contrast, China and Vietnam display low poverty rates around 30% despite having democracy scores below 0.2. The data is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices, relating to income measured after taxes and benefits or to consumption, per capita. The distribution shows a dense cluster of points in the lower-right area, indicating that many nations combine relatively high democratic scores with low poverty levels.
